title: Search at bounded speed with immigration
abstract: Many biophysical search processes employ searchers which enter or "immigrate" into the domain progressively over time. Existing search time estimates can become unphysical for fast immigration since they imply that searchers move at infinite speed. In this paper, we investigate search times of immigrating searchers that move at bounded speed. In the fast immigration limit, we determine the full probability distribution and all the moments of the $k$th search time in terms of the early time probability distribution of a single searcher. We apply these rigorous mathematical results to several canonical models of stochastic search. We further analyze different models of "diffusion" and use these results to investigate when and how the minutiae of searcher dynamics affect search times. We compare our theory to numerical simulations.
summary: This paper develops rigorous mathematical results for estimating the time required for a search process to find a target when searchers arrive continuously (immigrate). It extends existing theory to account for searchers moving at a bounded speed, deriving exact distributions and moments in the fast immigration limit. The results are applied to canonical models like Brownian motion and run-and-tumble dynamics.
main_problem: The paper investigates the search times for searchers that progressively enter or 'immigrate' into a domain at a constant Poissonian rate, specifically addressing the case where searchers move at a bounded speed.
main_result: In the fast immigration limit, the probability distribution and moments of the $k$-th search time ($T_k$) are determined, showing convergence to Erlang distributions.
method: The analysis uses properties of inhomogeneous Poisson point processes and derives survival probabilities based on the short-time behavior of the single-searcher first passage time distribution.
model_or_system: The models analyzed include 1D, 2D, and 3D run-and-tumble processes, Brownian motion, and linear PDMPs, all subjected to a constant immigration rate ($\lambda$).
key_observables: The $k$-th First Passage Time ($T_k$) and its moments ($E[T_k^m]$).
important_parameters: Immigration rate ($\lambda$), system size ($L$), searcher speed ($v$), and the characteristic time scale ($t_{\min} = L/v$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is rigorously performed in the fast immigration limit ($\lambda 	o \infty$) and assumes the underlying single-searcher process ($	au$) has a specific short-time distribution.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the relative error of theoretical approximations against stochastic simulations for four different search models. Figure 2 compares the mean first passage time across three processes (BM, discrete, run-and-tumble) showing convergence/divergence regimes.
paper_structure: The paper develops general theorems for the fast immigration limit, applies these theorems to various physical models (run-and-tumble, BM), and compares the theoretical predictions against numerical simulations, paying close attention to the breakdown of approximations in different parameter regimes.
why_interesting: While the core topic is stochastic search, the mathematical machinery involving convergence theorems, moment calculations, and the comparison between different physical diffusion models (BM vs. run-and-tumble) is highly relevant to non-equilibrium statistical physics and transport phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison of theory of section 3 with stochastic simulations of four search models. The black curves plot the relative error for approximations to the mean fFPT. The red curves plot the error for the approximations to the full distribution of the fFPT, where the error is the Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance (i.e. the maximum absolute difference between the cumulative distribution func- tions).
Figure 2: Comparison of the mean fFPTs for the three “diffusion” processes of sections 5.1-5.3.
